result:
Late at night the city exhales a warm, metallic breath that smells of electricity and wet asphalt. I like to walk barefoot across the pedestrian bridge just to feel the tremor of traffic in my ankles, as if the river of taillights is a second bloodstream. Somewhere below, a saxophone repeats four notes that never resolve; they hover like moths around a streetlamp, refusing to land. I adopt them as my heartbeat for the next twenty steps, then abandon them to the wind.  

On the opposite bank I discover a vending machine selling tiny glass planets for two euros each. I insert coins until my palm holds Saturn, Neptune, and a cracked Earth missing Australia. The planets are cold, heavier than their size suggests, as though gravity itself has been distilled. I pocket them, suddenly responsible for three orbits.  

A cat with a white question-mark tail follows me through three alleys, always keeping the same two-meter distance. When I stop, it stops; when I cough, it blinks. We negotiate without language the treaty of not-needing. At the fourth alley it sits, washes its face, and declares the conversation over.  

By the time the sky begins to pale, my pockets clink like distant wind chimes. I realize I have become a portable cosmos, a rogue galaxy migrating on foot. The saxophone’s last note finally lands inside my chest, soft as a comma, promising the sentence will continue tomorrow. I agree, turn home, and let the city inhale me again.

By the time the sky begins to pale, my pockets clink like distant wind chimes. I realize I have become a portable cosmos, a rogue galaxy migrating on foot.
